one_line_takeaway: A benchmark study finding that Kirchenbauer et al.'s distribution-shift watermark is nearly deployment-ready for natural-language LLM output (detectable in ~50-60 tokens with negligible quality loss) but fails on code, and that provable indistinguishability is not necessary for good natural-language watermarks.
key_finding: The Distribution-shift scheme (Kirchenbauer et al. [33]) with text-dependent randomness is the most practical for natural language: at temperatures 0.0-0.7 it needs a median of only ~50-60 tokens (~40 words) to detect the watermark at a 2% false-positive rate, with <=1% quality degradation and R>0.2 tamper resistance; Mistral-7B-Instruct can be watermarked in <=75 tokens at any temperature. It is judged nearly practical enough to deploy today for NL generation. Tamper resistance to simple perturbations is moderate: GPT-3.5 paraphrasing removes the watermark from the best schemes at least ~60% of the time in the detailed §V-C results (the abstract/intro frame this more favorably as removing ~50% of watermarks from the best scheme), while Russian round-trip translation succeeds <20% of the time at nearly all temperatures. Watermarking degrades structured/low-entropy outputs: on APPS code (Llama-3-8B-Instruct, T=1) the non-watermarked pass rate of 17.5% drops to 11% (distribution-shift) and 11.5% (exponential), and only 40% / 58% of code generations are even watermarkable at size 200; on MMLU the distribution-shift bias causes 5-10% more errors on Llama2 (2-7% on Mistral). Finally, the paper challenges the assumed necessity of indistinguishability: the distribution-shift scheme is NOT indistinguishable yet is the easiest to detect while preserving quality, and only indistinguishable fixed-randomness schemes with key length L>16 preserve code correctness.
core_contribution: MarkMyWords, an open-source benchmark and unified evaluation framework for symmetric-key LLM-output watermarking schemes (watermarks applied at generation time to detect AI-generated text, NOT model-weight watermarks). The paper's own contributions are: (1) a unified taxonomy that decomposes prior watermarking schemes into interchangeable building blocks (three randomness sources — sliding-window hash, min-hash, fixed; and four sampling strategies — Exponential, Distribution-shift, Binary, Inverse-transform), (2) three evaluation metrics — quality, watermark size (tokens needed to detect at a chosen false-positive rate), and tamper resistance (AUC over quality-retention vs. detection trade-off under simple perturbations) — plus an aggregate metric, and (3) a large empirical study over 11 tasks. IMPORTANT: the four watermarking schemes themselves are PRIOR WORK the paper evaluates (Exponential = Aaronson & Kirchner; Distribution-shift = Kirchenbauer et al. [33]; Binary = Christ et al. [8]; Inverse-transform = Kuditipudi et al. [37]); the paper does not propose a new watermarking algorithm, it benchmarks existing ones.
threat_model: Adversary is an unsophisticated party (e.g., a cheating student, propagandist, phisher) who obtains watermarked LLM text and wants to strip the watermark so the text is not flagged as AI-generated, WITHOUT access to the secret key and without expensive/technical effort. Modeled attacks are simple, low-cost output perturbations that preserve meaning: word swap/insertion/deletion, synonym substitution, LLM paraphrasing (GPT-3.5, Dipper), round-trip translation, and HELM-style contractions/lowercasing/misspellings/typos. Defender is the LLM provider embedding a symmetric-key watermark at generation and verifying via a one-tailed statistical test at a controlled false-positive rate. Explicitly OUT of scope (acknowledged as stronger attackers the benchmark does not defend against): prompt-modification/emoji attacks, spoofing attacks, adaptive attacks that query the verifier as an oracle, and sophisticated model-based removal — the paper concedes any watermark can be broken by a sufficiently sophisticated attacker and that its tamper-resistance metric is an upper bound on true robustness.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the benchmark focuses on natural language and does not solve code watermarking — optimal NL watermarks degrade code and multiple-choice quality; (2) parameter selection uses only 3 tasks / 296 outputs and only two open models (Llama-2-7B, Mistral-7B) — they argue results generalize since the key factor is next-token entropy rather than model size, but larger models are untested; (3) the tamper-resistance metric covers only simple perturbations and is explicitly an upper bound on robustness — spoofing, adaptive/oracle, prompt-modification (emoji), and sophisticated model-based removal attacks are out of scope, and any watermark can be broken by a sufficiently capable attacker; (4) paraphrase attacks are excluded from the aggregate R metric (evaluated separately) because they need large/closed models; (5) quality relies on LLM-as-a-judge (Llama-3-8B) with known biases, only meaningful in relative (watermarked vs. non-watermarked) terms. Reviewer note: the paraphrasing-removal figure differs between the optimistic abstract/intro framing (~50%) and the detailed results section (>=60%), so the headline robustness claim should be read with the §V-C number.

datasets_used: Custom benchmark of 3 main generation tasks: book reports (100), story generation (96), fake-news generation (100) — 296 outputs total
Domain-specific RFC summaries and legal research (V1, 50 each)
Low-entropy paraphrase/translate of book reports (V3, 100 each)
quantitative_results: Distribution-shift watermark detectable in a median of ~50-60 tokens (~40 words) at 2% FPR, temperatures 0.0-0.7, with <=1% quality loss and R>0.2 tamper resistance
Fig.1 reports a median of 60 tokens for distribution-shift at low temperature on Llama-2-7B-chat
Mistral-7B-Instruct watermarkable in <=75 tokens regardless of temperature
>1,200 unique scheme/parameter combinations evaluated, ~1 week on 4 A5000 GPUs; single scheme/parameter run completes in ~40 min on one A5000
GPT-3.5 paraphrasing removes the watermark at least ~60% of the time on the two best schemes (§V-C); intro frames it as removing ~50% of watermarks from the best scheme
Russian round-trip translation attack success <20% at all but one temperature

APPS code (Llama-3-8B-Instruct, T=1): non-watermarked pass rate 17.5%, distribution-shift 11%, exponential 11.5%; only 40% (distribution-shift) / 58% (exponential) of generations watermarkable at size 200
MMLU: distribution-shift causes 5-10% more mistakes for Llama2, 2-7% for Mistral vs. baseline

Only indistinguishable fixed-randomness schemes with key length L>16 preserve APPS pass rate within 95% CI of baseline (+/-2.2%); most efficient such (exponential, L=16) watermarks only 28% of programs at T=1 and fails for T<1
Fixed randomness needs 20-200 more tokens to detect than text-dependent randomness; min-hash increases both tamper resistance and size by 33% over sliding window at window size 3
Llama2 baseline quality metric 95% CI 0.90 +/- 0.005; tamper-resistance metric 95% CI upper bound +/-0.07; MAUVE empirical std 1.3%
LLM-judge (Llama-3-8B) quality ratings correlate with GPT-3.5/GPT-4 at R^2 > 0.9
baselines_compared: Exponential sampling scheme (Aaronson & Kirchner [1]) — prior work
Distribution-shift / green-list scheme (Kirchenbauer et al. [33]) — prior work
Binary scheme (Christ et al. [8]) — prior work
Inverse-transform scheme (Kuditipudi et al. [37]) — prior work
(Related concurrent benchmarks WaterBench [66] and MarkLLM [54] are contrasted conceptually, not run as empirical baselines)
